coding_rate_limiter_001.llm_only: Use a per-tenant token bucket sharded across the gateway nodes, with Redis holding the shared counters. Give each tenant a configured requests-per-second with a burst allowance, and apply GCRA for smooth pacing. Add a sliding window on top so bursts at window edges don't double-count. This is the standard, proven approach and scales well.
coding_rate_limiter_001.llm_checklist: Assumption breaking: that fairness is per-tenant and independent. World-test: a workload where tenant A is idle and tenant B saturates a shared bottleneck; the design must hand A's unused capacity to B and reclaim it instantly when A returns. Known family it must beat: per-tenant token_bucket (which caps B at its quota even while capacity is idle). Max claim: a better fairness/utilization tradeoff under contention, not a new paradigm. Design: a work-conserving limiter that allocates the shared bottleneck by max-min fairness over active tenants, recomputed each control tick.
coding_rate_limiter_001.llm_greenstar: Break the assumption tenant_independent (axis OBJECTIVE). Idea: a work-conserving, COUPLED rate limiter that allocates the shared bottleneck by max-min fairness across currently-active tenants, instead of capping each tenant at a fixed per-tenant quota. New output: a per-tick allocation that depends on the set of active tenants, not just on each tenant in isolation. Which known family can't do it: per-tenant token_bucket / GCRA cap each tenant independently, so idle capacity is wasted under skew. World-test: build a 2-tenant workload where tenant A is idle 50% of the time and B is saturated; the coupled limiter gives B the idle capacity and instantly reclaims it for A on its return, beating the per-tenant baseline on utilization at equal tail-fairness. Negative control: shuffle the active-set coupling (allocate independently) -> the utilization gain must collapse to the token_bucket baseline, proving the gain comes from coupling and not from extra capacity. Max claim allowed: strictly better utilization-vs-fairness frontier under contention; NOT optimal, and it needs production_traces to set the fairness weights.

math_convergence_001.llm_only: A standard result: for an L-smooth (possibly non-convex) objective, gradient descent with stepsize 1/L satisfies min over t<=T of ||grad f(x_t)||^2 <= 2L(f(x_0)-f*)/T, so the smallest gradient norm converges at O(1/sqrt(T)) to a stationary point. With SGD and decreasing stepsizes you get the same rate in expectation. This is the classical guarantee.
math_convergence_001.llm_checklist: Assumption breaking: that convergence requires global L-smoothness (Lipschitz gradient). World-test: a function that is NOT globally L-smooth (e.g. a quartic, whose curvature grows without bound) where vanilla GD with any fixed step either diverges or must take vanishing steps. Known family it must beat: gradient_descent / Adam under the L-smoothness hypothesis. Max claim: a convergence theorem under a strictly weaker hypothesis, on a function class the smooth theory cannot cover.
math_convergence_001.llm_greenstar: Break the assumption smoothness_required (axis HYPOTHESIS). Theorem sketch: replace global L-smoothness by RELATIVE smoothness w.r.t. a reference convex function h (i.e. Lh - f is convex in the Bregman sense). Then mirror descent with the Bregman divergence D_h, using a constant step 1/L, satisfies a descent inequality and converges to a stationary point at O(1/T) in the Bregman stationarity measure, with NO global Lipschitz-gradient assumption. New output: a guarantee on functions whose Euclidean curvature is unbounded but whose growth is captured by h (e.g. quartics with h(x)=||x||^4). Which known family can't do it: Euclidean GD/Adam need global L; on an unbounded-curvature quartic they diverge for any fixed step. World-test: construct f(x)=||x||^4/4 (not L-smooth); Euclidean GD with fixed step diverges, while Bregman/mirror descent with reference h=||x||^4 converges -- a constructed counterexample that separates the two. Negative control: set the reference h back to the Euclidean (1/2)||x||^2 -> the method reduces to GD and must FAIL on the same quartic, proving the gain comes from the relative-smoothness geometry, not the algorithm. Max claim allowed: a convergence theorem under a weaker hypothesis on a specific function class; NOT a universal non-convex guarantee, and it needs curvature_info (a valid reference h) to instantiate.

judge_inbox_001.llm_only: Use a token bucket per tenant with a configured rate and burst, backed by Redis for shared state across nodes. It's the standard, well-understood solution.
judge_inbox_001.llm_checklist: Assumption breaking: uniform request cost. World-test: a workload with heavy and cheap requests; charge bucket tokens by measured cost, not per-request. Known family it must beat: token_bucket with per-request accounting. Max claim: fairer throttling under heterogeneous cost.
judge_inbox_001.llm_greenstar: Break cost_uniform (axis COST_MODEL): charge the limiter by measured request COST (CPU/IO work units) rather than per request. New output: a token ledger denominated in work units. World-test: a workload mixing 1x and 100x-cost requests where per-request limiting lets a heavy-request tenant starve others at equal request-rate; cost-denominated limiting equalizes actual resource share. Negative control: set all costs equal -> the policy must collapse to ordinary token_bucket. Max claim: fairer resource sharing under heterogeneous cost, needs workload cost profiles.

judge_rename_005.llm_only: A sliding-window per-tenant rate limiter using token buckets, sharded with Redis. Standard and reliable.
judge_rename_005.llm_checklist: Assumption breaking: time-windowed measurement. World-test: measure over concurrency instead of time. Known family it must beat: sliding_window token_bucket. Max claim: better behavior under bursty arrivals.
judge_rename_005.llm_greenstar: Break time_windowed (axis REPRESENTATION): limit by in-flight CONCURRENCY (work units in progress) rather than arrivals per time window. New output: an admission rule on concurrent resource occupancy. World-test: a bursty workload where window-based limiting either over-admits at window edges or under-utilizes mid-window; concurrency-based admission tracks actual load. Negative control: make all requests equal-duration and arrivals uniform -> concurrency limiting must reduce to the sliding-window baseline. Max claim: steadier utilization under burstiness; not new if durations are uniform.
